result: I keep thinking about the small, soft hours—those nameless stretches between three and five in the morning when the world is half-asleep and half-awake, like a cat dozing with one eye open. Streetlights hum themselves hoarse; a refrigerator clicks off somewhere down the hall, satisfied that the milk is still cold. Nothing important is supposed to happen then, which is exactly why everything feels possible. I’ve written my clearest sentences at that time, the ink almost surprised to land on the page. Outside, the air smells of wet pavement and the last of the lilacs, a combination that reminds me of being seven and convinced that ghosts lived in the hedge. I still believe they do, just friendlier ones who’ve learned to keep quiet. Sometimes I open the window and let the sound of nothing rush in; it has the same texture as the silence my grandmother left behind when she forgot how to speak. I carry it around all day like a pebble in my shoe, unnoticed until I’m halfway through a grocery line and suddenly limp with memory. People glance over, but no one asks; we all pretend we’re intact. The checkout girl twirls her barcode scanner like a tiny light-saber, beeping items into existence. I pay for bananas and dish soap, drive home, and microwave yesterday’s coffee. Somewhere in the interval, the small hours evaporate, but their residue lingers on my fingertips, a salt-of-the-moon film that makes everything I touch briefly luminous, as though the world were new and had not yet decided what it wanted to be.

Streetlights hum themselves hoarse; a refrigerator clicks off somewhere down the hall, satisfied that the milk is still cold. Nothing important is supposed to happen then, which is exactly why everything feels possible. I’ve written my clearest sentences at that time, the ink almost surprised to land on the page. Outside, the air smells of wet pavement and the last of the lilacs, a combination that reminds me of being seven and convinced that ghosts lived in the hedge. I still believe they do, just friendlier ones who’ve learned to keep quiet. Sometimes I open the window and let the sound of nothing rush in; it has the same texture as the silence my grandmother left behind when she forgot how to speak. I carry it around all day like a pebble in my shoe, unnoticed until I’m halfway through a grocery line and suddenly limp with memory. People glance over, but no one asks; we all pretend we’re intact. The checkout girl twirls her barcode scanner like a tiny light-saber, beeping items into existence. I pay for bananas and dish soap, drive home, and microwave yesterday’s coffee. Somewhere in the interval, the small hours evaporate, but their residue lingers on my fingertips, a salt-of-the-moon film that makes everything I touch briefly luminous, as though the world were new and had not yet decided what it wanted to be.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
